findings_details.title: RETURN trap leaks to caller scopes
findings_details.body: `trap ... RETURN` set inside `_rq_migrate_legacy_state` is not function-local in bash by default — it fires on every caller return up the stack (verified: inner sets trap, outer also fires it on its own return). In practice the first fire of `rmdir '$lock'` succeeds and subsequent fires are silenced by `2>/dev/null || true`, so no data loss occurs. However the trap definition persists in ancestor scopes and can interfere with unrelated RETURN traps added later in `resolve_quality_dir`/`resolve_state_path` or their callers. Fix: declare `local -` at the top of `_rq_migrate_legacy_state` (saves/restores shell options and trap state in bash 4.4+) OR save the existing RETURN trap, install the new one, and restore on exit. Simpler still: skip the trap entirely and `rmdir` explicitly on both the mv-success and mv-failure paths — only two exit points after the lock is acquired.

findings_details.title: _resolve_qa_state_dir delegates to rq-state-path.sh without repo-key derivation — produces basename-only path, not owner-repo keyed
findings_details.body: rq-state-path.sh uses the repo-key.sh <owner>-<repo> scheme (EPIC #395). _lib.sh calls it correctly via --cross-branch, so the path is correct. However the therapist reference (_lib_paths.sh) explicitly guards that rq-state-path.sh is found in $HOME/.claude (never `find .`) to prevent a crafted repo from injecting arbitrary bash. _lib.sh uses a relative path `../../rq/scripts/rq-state-path.sh` rooted at the plugin cache — this is safe only because _QA_LIB_SCRIPT_DIR is derived from BASH_SOURCE[0] at cache installation time, not from cwd. This is fine in practice but the therapist pattern is more defensive. No fix required — note for parity audit.

findings_details.title: find_matching_run accumulates all matching lines into $matches with string concatenation — O(N) string growth for large runs.jsonl
findings_details.body: The function reads every line of runs.jsonl into a shell string variable via `matches="$matches$parsed"$'\n'`. For repos with thousands of QA runs this becomes quadratic. The final `tail -n 1` needs only the last match. Fix: replace the accumulation pattern with a last-match-wins read that overwrites a single variable, discarding all but the most recent match. This eliminates the O(N) string and the final grep/tail pipeline.

findings_details.title: find_matching_run reads from $QA_STATE_DIR/runs.jsonl without checking file existence — parent caller checks, but function contract is silent
findings_details.body: resolve_comparison() guards `[ ! -f "$QA_STATE_DIR/runs.jsonl" ]` before calling find_matching_run, so the missing-file case is handled. But find_matching_run itself has no guard — a direct call bypasses the check and the `while read` silently produces no output rather than erroring. The function should either document that callers must verify the file exists, or add its own guard with a clear error. A comment on the function header would suffice.

findings_details.title: emit_observations uses sort -z on find -print0 output — but sort -z is not portable on macOS (BSD sort does not support -z)
findings_details.body: Line 110: `find -L ... -print0 | sort -z` — macOS `sort` (BSD) does NOT support `-z` (NUL-delimited sort). This will fail silently or produce unsorted output on macOS. The same `find ... -print0 | sort -z` pattern appears in the initial blob build at line 75. Fix: replace with `find ... | sort | ...` using newline-separated filenames (acceptable since .md observation filenames won't contain newlines), or use a python3/awk shim for NUL sort.

findings_details.title: Initial blob build also uses find ... -print0 | sort -z (same BSD sort -z portability issue)
findings_details.body: Same macOS portability issue as line 110. The TMP_OBS build at line 73-76 uses the same pattern. Both sites need the same fix.

findings_details.title: check_secrets_in_qa pipes grep output through head -5 — truncating hit list could mask additional secrets
findings_details.body: Line 205-207: `grep ... | grep -vF 'qa-secrets-ok' | head -5 || true`. The `head -5` cap means if there are 6+ secrets, only 5 are shown in the error. More critically, the `|| true` on the pipeline means grep's exit code is swallowed even when there are 0 matches (grep -r exits 1 on no match). The current code works because `[ -z "$hits" ]` correctly detects no-match, but the `|| true` on a pipeline with `head` causes a pipefail violation on macOS when head closes the pipe early. Fix: remove `head -5` (or store without truncation) and capture exit code explicitly.

findings_details.title: _warn_if_git_tracked uses basename of source_dir as the git ls-files argument — will not find tracked files when run from outside repo root
findings_details.body: Line 109: `rel_path=$(basename "$source_dir")` then `git ls-files "$rel_path/"`. `git ls-files` takes paths relative to the CWD, not relative to the repo root. If the script is invoked from a subdirectory, `git ls-files ".qa/"` will find nothing even when .qa/ is tracked at the repo root. Fix: use the path relative to the repo root, which is already computed: `rel_path="${source_dir#"$repo_root/"}"`. The `repo_root` is available in `main()` but not passed to this helper — it should be passed as an argument or derived inside the function.

findings_details.title: --qa deprecation warning references issue #434 via bare fragment — should use full URL per url-discipline.md
findings_details.body: Line 139: `see https://github.com/arqu-co/agent-plugins/issues/434` — this is already a full URL, compliant. Actually the --therapist warning on line 133 also uses a full URL. Both are fine. This finding is retracted.

findings_details.title: printf -- '- [%s] %s\n' is correct but the -- is unnecessary for printf (not rm)
findings_details.body: printf does not interpret -- as an end-of-options marker; it is harmless but noisy. The convention for printf is just `printf '- [%s] %s\n'`. Minor style point, not a defect.

findings_details.title: QA_STATE_DIR_OVERRIDE accepted without path validation — path traversal possible
findings_details.body: QA_STATE_DIR_OVERRIDE is documented as a test escape hatch but is accepted without any validation. Any value — including '../../etc', paths with shell metacharacters, or paths outside the repo — is used verbatim. A malicious or misconfigured environment can redirect all QA state I/O (observations, runs.jsonl) to an arbitrary location. Fix: validate that QA_STATE_DIR_OVERRIDE is an absolute path and does not contain '..' components. Warn to stderr if it points outside the repo tree. At minimum: [[ $QA_STATE_DIR_OVERRIDE =~ ^\/ ]] || { echo 'QA_STATE_DIR_OVERRIDE must be an absolute path' >&2; exit 1; }

findings_details.title: rq-state-path.sh resolved with relative path that assumes _QA_LIB_SCRIPT_DIR layout
findings_details.body: state_path_sh is set to _QA_LIB_SCRIPT_DIR/../../rq/scripts/rq-state-path.sh. This works only when the qa plugin is installed at exactly two directory levels below rq/scripts. If the plugin is installed anywhere else (e.g. via symlink, alternative cache path), the path silently resolves to the wrong location or doesn't exist, and the fallback is $(pwd)/.qa-state — which is wrong for production use. Fix: use the canonical PLUGIN_DIR discover pattern (find . -name rq-state-path.sh) with a HOME/.claude fallback, mirroring how all other scripts in this repo discover helpers.

findings_details.title: find_matching_run reads from QA_STATE_DIR/runs.jsonl without checking QA_STATE_DIR is non-empty
findings_details.body: find_matching_run opens $QA_STATE_DIR/runs.jsonl directly. If QA_STATE_DIR is an empty string (which can happen when rq-state-path.sh fails and the fallback also silently returns empty), the redirection becomes </runs.jsonl which either reads from a file in cwd or causes an error under set -euo pipefail. The check at line 221 ([ ! -f "$QA_STATE_DIR/runs.jsonl" ]) comes in resolve_comparison, but find_matching_run can be called with an empty QA_STATE_DIR if the caller logic ever changes. Fix: add a guard at the top of find_matching_run: [ -n "$QA_STATE_DIR" ] || { echo 'qa-compare: QA_STATE_DIR is unset' >&2; return 1; }

findings_details.title: patch_proof temp file written to system mktemp location, not alongside target — mv across filesystems will fail
findings_details.body: tmp=$(mktemp) creates the temp file in /tmp (or TMPDIR). Then mv "$tmp" "$target". If target is on a different filesystem from TMPDIR (e.g. a network mount or a different disk), mv will fail with 'Invalid cross-device link'. Fix: use mktemp in the same directory as target: tmp=$(mktemp "$(dirname "$target")/qa-compare.XXXXXX").

findings_details.title: list_observations_oldest_first uses xargs -I{} without null delimiter — breaks on filenames with spaces
findings_details.body: printf '%s\n' "$files" | xargs -I{} stat ... {} — this splits on newlines and is vulnerable to filenames containing spaces or newlines. observation filenames are validated to be kebab-case slugs (by qa-observe.sh validate_slug) so spaces are prevented for new files, but older files or files created outside the script could still break this. Additionally, $files is set with $(...) which itself strips trailing newlines. Fix: use find -print0 + xargs -0 for null-safe stat, matching the pattern already used in the emit_observations function at line 107.

findings_details.title: check_dev_server passes --url value directly to curl without scheme validation
findings_details.body: URL is accepted from --url argument and passed directly to curl -fsS ... "$url". No scheme validation is performed. A value like 'file:///etc/passwd' or 'dict://...', 'gopher://...' would be passed to curl, which supports many schemes. In a CI/automated context this could be used to exfiltrate data or probe internal services. Fix: validate URL starts with http:// or https:// before passing to curl: case "$url" in http://*|https://*) ;; *) echo 'qa-preflight: --url must be http:// or https://' >&2; exit 2 ;; esac

findings_details.title: _copy_dir_to_root does not verify copy succeeded before rm -rf source
findings_details.body: The migration sequence is: tar copy to dest_root, then rm -rf source_dir (line 88), then mkdir + ln -s. There is no verification that the tar copy completed successfully. If tar fails mid-copy (disk full, permission error), the error is swallowed by set -e only if tar exits non-zero — but (cd ... && tar cf - .) | (cd ... && tar xf -) in a pipe means tar xf - failure does NOT cause the outer script to exit under set -e because it's the right side of a pipe. PIPEFAIL is set, so the script should exit, but that still means rm -rf runs after a partial copy on some shell versions. Fix: capture tar exit status explicitly or use cp -a for the copy, and verify the destination has the expected content before removing source.

findings_details.title: _warn_if_git_tracked uses basename of source_dir for git ls-files — wrong for nested paths
findings_details.body: rel_path=$(basename "$source_dir") strips all directory components. Then git ls-files "$rel_path/" runs in the current working directory (wherever the script was called from), not from repo root. For example if source_dir is /repo/plugins/.quality, basename gives .quality and git ls-files .quality/ runs from cwd, not from repo root — missing tracked files. Fix: compute rel_path using git rev-parse --show-toplevel and Python or parameter expansion to get the path relative to the repo root, and run git ls-files from the repo root.

findings_details.title: ensure_topic_file writes to observations/ subdir but only mkdir is done before the loop, not checked per-topic
findings_details.body: Line 100 calls mkdir -p $QA_STATE_DIR/observations before the loop. This is correct for the happy path. However if ensure_qa_state_dir is called and QA_STATE_DIR exists but observations/ subdir somehow got removed between script start and ensure_topic_file being called (e.g. concurrent deletion), there is no per-call mkdir guard in ensure_topic_file. This is a TOCTOU window. Severity is low because it requires concurrent deletion. The mkdir -p at line 100 is the right fix location — it already exists. This is informational, no fix needed beyond documenting the assumption.

findings_details.title: CWD-rooted find sources untrusted repo-key.sh on every therapist hook
findings_details.body: _th_source_repo_key runs find . -name repo-key.sh -path */rq/scripts/lib/* | head -1 before falling back to /Users/jerrod/.claude. Therapist hooks run automatically on every Claude Code turn regardless of repo. An attacker who gets a user to cd into (or clone) a repo containing plugins/rq/scripts/lib/repo-key.sh (or any path matching */rq/scripts/lib/*/repo-key.sh) achieves arbitrary bash execution via source. Unlike rq skill preambles (deliberately invoked), therapist hooks are automatic, so CWD searching is a code-execution sink. Fix: remove the CWD branch (find . ...) entirely; search only /Users/jerrod/.claude/plugins and a path derived from BASH_SOURCE (the sibling plugin cache). Keep the inline fallback for the no-rq-installed case.

findings_details.title: Trailing-slash remote URL yields trailing-dash key
findings_details.body: For a remote URL like https://github.com/acme/widgets/ (trailing slash, uncommon but possible from manual git remote set-url), derivation yields owner_repo=widgets/ → after s|/|-|g → 'widgets-'. _rq_is_safe_key accepts 'widgets-' (regex allows trailing dash). The resulting state dir /Users/jerrod/.claude/state/widgets-/... works but is ugly and differs from the same repo accessed via https://github.com/acme/widgets.git (→ acme-widgets). This causes the same repo to key into two different state dirs depending on how the remote was set. Fix: after scheme strip, trim trailing slashes from trimmed before splitting (trimmed=${trimmed%/}). Add a test_repo_key.sh case.

findings_details.title: No adversarial test cases for path-traversal-ish remote URLs
findings_details.body: test_repo_key.sh covers happy-path GitHub/GitLab/SSH/file-URL parsing but no adversarial inputs: git@host:../evil/repo.git, git@host:-evil/repo.git, URLs with embedded NUL via basename, URLs containing whitespace, trailing-slash URLs, and URLs that would produce an unsafe key (must be caught by _rq_is_safe_key downstream). The whole point of centralizing this in lib/repo-key.sh is to make it the security-trust boundary — tests should prove the boundary holds. Add: assertions that unsafe inputs produce keys which _rq_is_safe_key rejects, and/or direct assertions that derive returns a safe key string.

findings_details.title: Inline fallback duplicates derive logic — divergence risk
findings_details.body: The inline fallback (lines 25-51) reproduces _rq_is_safe_key and _rq_derive_repo_key byte-identically from lib/repo-key.sh. When the canonical lib changes (e.g. fixing the trailing-slash key, tightening the safe-key regex), the inline copy will silently diverge — the whole point of issue #441 was to eliminate exactly this duplication. There is no parity test that runs both the sourced path and the fallback path against a fixture matrix. Fix: either (a) add a test that force-unsets rq discovery and runs the fallback against test_repo_key.sh's fixture matrix, or (b) ship the repo-key.sh with the therapist plugin directly so the 'fallback' IS the sourced file and there is only one copy.

findings_details.title: Stale lock from crashed invocation permanently blocks migration
findings_details.body: If a process is killed (SIGKILL, SIGTERM without trap, power loss, or any uncaught set -e abort from a caller sourcing this lib) between `mkdir "$lock"` on line 61 and `rmdir "$lock"` on lines 64/70/74, the lock directory persists forever. Every subsequent call hits `mkdir "$lock" 2>/dev/null || return 0` and silently no-ops — migration becomes permanently impossible for that repo/key on that machine with no user-visible error. The legacy state dir sits there forever, and the hot path continues to run the lock acquisition on every hook invocation. Recommended fixes (one or more): (a) add a `trap 'rmdir "$lock" 2>/dev/null' EXIT INT TERM` inside the function scope after mkdir succeeds so signal-based exits clean up; (b) add stale-lock age recovery before `return 0` — e.g. if `mkdir` fails, check the lock's mtime and rmdir+retry if older than some threshold (60s is ample — migration is a single mv); (c) at minimum, emit a stderr warning the first time migration is skipped due to lock contention so operators can diagnose.

findings_details.title: No EXIT/INT/TERM trap means every non-normal exit leaks the lock
findings_details.body: The three in-function cleanup calls (`rmdir "$lock"` on lines 64, 70, 74) only fire on the three explicit code paths. Any other termination — the caller's `set -e` aborting a subsequent command after this function returns but while this function's frame is still on the stack is not applicable (bash functions don't defer), but signal delivery during `mv` (line 73) absolutely is. SIGTERM/SIGINT during the `mv` — which for a large legacy state tree across filesystems can take many seconds — kills the script mid-mv with the lock still held. Next invocation permanently no-ops (see companion finding on line 61). Fix: install a function-scoped trap immediately after the `mkdir "$lock"` succeeds: `trap 'rmdir "$lock" 2>/dev/null || true' RETURN INT TERM` and clear it before normal return. Bash RETURN traps fire even on early returns.

findings_details.title: Pre-emptive lock-directory creation by a local process blocks migration
findings_details.body: A process with write access to `$HOME/.claude/state/` — realistically the same user, but also any buggy tool, container-shared-home setup, or a prior crashed rq invocation — can create `$HOME/.claude/state/.migrate-<new_key>.lock` as a directory. From that moment forward, `_rq_migrate_legacy_state` silently returns 0 on every call and the legacy state tree is never migrated. Impact is limited because an attacker with `$HOME` write already owns the user, but the crashed-invocation variant is the realistic DoS path (see the stale-lock finding). Mitigation overlaps with the stale-lock fix: age-based lock recovery with a stderr warning eliminates both the crash-induced DoS and the pre-emptive-attacker DoS. Additionally consider tightening the lock path with `$$` or a random suffix used only as a probe, then renaming to the canonical lock — but the simpler stale-age check is sufficient.

findings_details.title: mv failure is silently swallowed with no diagnostic
findings_details.body: `mv "$old_root" "$new_root" 2>/dev/null || true` hides real failures (EXDEV cross-filesystem, EACCES, ENOSPC). The lock is released, so the next caller retries and fails identically in perpetuity — user sees no indication that their legacy state is stranded. Unlike the race-loss case (handled earlier by the re-check), a genuine mv failure deserves a one-shot stderr warning: `mv ... || { echo "warn: failed to migrate $old_root → $new_root" >&2; rmdir "$lock"; return 0; }`. This also makes the `|| true` on mv unnecessary.

findings_details.title: _warn_if_git_tracked uses relative path — git ls-files gives wrong result when cwd != repo root
findings_details.body: rel_path is set via basename($source_dir) and then passed to git ls-files "$rel_path/" as a relative path. The function is called from _migrate_dir which is called from main() without first cd-ing to $repo_root. If the caller's cwd is not the repo root, git ls-files silently returns nothing (false negative: no warning fires when it should) or returns wrong results. Fix: pass --full-name and run git ls-files from $repo_root, or cd to repo_root before the ls-files call.

findings_details.title: head -n "-${keep}" fails on macOS BSD head — rotate_journal_if_needed crashes
findings_details.body: BSD head (macOS) does not support negative line counts: 'head: illegal line count -- -5'. The call head -n "-${keep}" on line 88 will exit non-zero on any macOS host when the journal exceeds MAX_JOURNAL_LINES. Since set -euo pipefail is active in _lib.sh callers, this kills the hook process. Fix: replace with head -n "$((MAX_JOURNAL_LINES / 2))" which achieves the intended archive of the first 5000 lines and is portable.

findings_details.title: lockf macOS branch: >> redirection applies to lockf, not to printf subprocess — journal append is silently lost
findings_details.body: Line 261: lockf -k -s -t 5 "$lock_file" printf '%s\n' "$entry" >>"${THERAPIST_DIR}/journal.jsonl". The >> operator redirects lockf's own stdout to the file, not the stdout of the printf child process lockf spawns. lockf forks printf as a new process whose stdout is the inherited fd from before the redirection — i.e., the hook output pipe or terminal. The journal write is silently dropped. Fix: lockf -k -s -t 5 "$lock_file" sh -c 'printf "%s\n" "$1" >> "$2"' _ "$entry" "${THERAPIST_DIR}/journal.jsonl"

findings_details.title: Repo-key derive+validate+migrate block duplicated between resolve_quality_dir and resolve_state_path
findings_details.body: After extracting _rq_current_branch_slug and _rq_resolve_state_key into lib/state-paths.sh, the two resolvers still duplicate ~8 lines of glue verbatim: capture remote_url, capture root, call _rq_derive_repo_key, validate via _rq_is_safe_key (with only the error prefix differing: 'quality-paths' vs 'rq-state-path'), compute legacy_key via basename on remote_url, and invoke _rq_migrate_legacy_state. Compare quality-paths.sh:42-57 to rq-state-path.sh:88-101 — the blocks are byte-identical apart from the prefix string. This is the same class of duplication that motivated #441/#448 and directly parallels the warn_prefix pattern already used by _rq_resolve_state_key. Fix: extract a third primitive in lib/state-paths.sh, e.g. _rq_prepare_repo_key <warn_prefix> that echoes the validated repo key and runs the legacy migration as a side effect, leaving callers with two lines (capture remote_url+root, call the helper). The parity test test_quality_paths_parity.sh continues to enforce byte-equal output.

findings_details.title: Unreachable root="" fallback after verify_git_worktree
findings_details.body: Line 91 assigns root="" on git rev-parse failure, but verify_git_worktree (line 79) has already exited with code 1 when we are not in a git worktree. git rev-parse --show-toplevel cannot fail here. The empty fallback is dead and also subtly unsafe: if it ever did fire, _rq_resolve_state_key would be called with binding="/.quality/branch-issue.json" (a literal absolute path), silently reading the wrong file rather than failing. Fix: drop the || root="" fallback and let -e surface any unexpected failure, matching resolve_quality_dir which does the same (quality-paths.sh:43-46) by returning 1 on a failed rev-parse instead of masking it.

findings_details.title: RETURN trap leaks into caller scope (and globally persists)
findings_details.body:
Evidence: Verified empirically with bash — a RETURN trap installed inside a function fires on EVERY subsequent function return in the caller chain and remains installed globally after the function exits. Reproducer: bash -c 'f(){ trap "echo X" RETURN; }; g(){ f; echo after; }; g; trap -p RETURN' prints X twice (once for f, once for g) and shows the trap still installed.

Impact: (1) rmdir of the lock path fires on every subsequent function return in resolve_quality_dir / resolve_state_path / _rq_prepare_repo_key — harmless no-ops but unexpected. (2) The trap PERSISTS after the migration call returns: any later code that inspects trap -p RETURN or relies on a clean trap table gets stale data. (3) If a future maintainer adds their own RETURN trap in a caller, the expectation that this one is scoped to _rq_migrate_legacy_state is false.

Fix: use 'local -' at the top of _rq_migrate_legacy_state (bash 4.4+; scopes shell options AND traps). Alternatively wrap the lock-taking body in a subshell ( ... ) so the trap dies with the subshell. Or explicitly clear: 'trap - RETURN' immediately before every return statement in this function. Given this file already targets bash (shell=bash), 'local -' is the cleanest.

findings_details.title: Race window between concurrent peers leaves new_root created-but-empty
findings_details.body:
Evidence: Peer A acquires the mkdir lock and begins mv. Peer B arrives within 60s, sees fresh lock, emits the 'held — retry on next invocation' warning and returns 0 at line 87. B's caller (_rq_prepare_repo_key) then returns, and its caller (resolve_quality_dir) proceeds to build $HOME/.claude/state/$repo/$branch_slug/rq and resolve_proof_dir / resolve_plans_dir call mkdir -p on it. That mkdir runs BEFORE A's mv completes, which now finds new_root already exists — A hits the [ -d "$new_root" ] guard at line 94 and emits 'both legacy and new state paths exist; not migrating'. Legacy state is stranded until manually moved.

Impact: whenever two hooks fire concurrently on a repo whose legacy state still exists, one of them guarantees the other's migration fails. Because the migration is one-shot and the warning is printed once per invocation, users hit this exactly when parallel sessions exist (the scenario #448 and its siblings target).

Fix: peer B should BLOCK on the lock (retry with backoff for some bounded duration) rather than returning 0 immediately, OR peer B's subsequent path construction must not mkdir -p under new_root until peer A releases the lock. Simplest: have peer B sleep+retry up to ~5s, then fall through with the current warning if A is genuinely stuck.

findings_details.title: _rq_prepare_repo_key name hides a filesystem-mutating side effect
findings_details.body:
Evidence: The name reads as a pure derivation helper ('prepare the key'), but it invokes _rq_migrate_legacy_state which performs an mv under $HOME/.claude/state. A reader skimming resolve_quality_dir line 48 has no syntactic cue that a rename may occur. The docstring at lines 43-47 does mention the side effect, but a function that moves directories on the user's home should wear that fact in its name.

Impact: future refactors (e.g. someone memoizing repo-key lookup, or adding a --dry-run path) may call _rq_prepare_repo_key expecting read-only behavior.

Fix: rename to something like _rq_prepare_repo_key_and_migrate, or split into _rq_derive_and_validate_repo_key (pure) + explicit _rq_migrate_legacy_state() call at each site. Given there are only two call sites (quality-paths.sh:48 and rq-state-path.sh:93), the explicit split is low-cost and removes the hidden-side-effect footgun.

findings_details.title: Bare issue ref in terminal-emitted deprecation warning
findings_details.body: The deprecation warning emitted to stderr uses 'arqu-co/agent-plugins#433' — a bare owner/repo#N ref. Per url-discipline.md, terminal output must use full https:// URLs. Users on mobile or remote surfaces cannot tap a bare ref. Fix: replace with https://github.com/arqu-co/agent-plugins/issues/433.

findings_details.title: Cross-reference comment contradicts the refactor
findings_details.body: Lines 13-17 still say: 'repo-key derivation logic lives in lib/repo-key.sh ... Any change to slugification, legacy-key migration, or branch-issue.json reading MUST be mirrored in rq-state-path.sh. The parity test test_quality_paths_parity.sh enforces byte-equal output across a fixture matrix'. That mirror-in-rq-state-path rule is exactly what #448 eliminates — slugification, legacy migration, and branch-issue.json reading now live once in lib/state-paths.sh and are sourced by both callers. Leaving this comment in place tells future maintainers to duplicate logic that is no longer duplicated, and name-drops a parity test whose raison d'etre has changed. Fix: rewrite the block to say state-key resolution and legacy-key migration live in lib/state-paths.sh (sourced by both resolve_quality_dir and rq-state-path.sh); keep only the perf note about not forking a subprocess from the hot path; drop or re-scope the parity-test reference to match current reality.

findings_details.title: Inlining rationale says 'duplicate' — PR collapses the duplication
findings_details.body: Lines 9-12 read: 'perf benchmarks showed p95 regressions up to +96% when this resolver forked to rq-state-path.sh. The hot path is exercised on every hook invocation, so we duplicate the small key/slug/migration logic in pure bash here.' The perf motivation is still correct — forking a subprocess is expensive — but after #448 the logic is NOT duplicated: it is sourced from lib/state-paths.sh into both resolve_quality_dir and rq-state-path.sh. Readers will be confused by 'we duplicate' pointing at code that no longer duplicates. Fix: reword to 'we source the shared primitives from lib/state-paths.sh rather than forking to rq-state-path.sh, keeping the hot path in-process.'

findings_details.title: Usage strings omit --read-pinned-key
findings_details.body: The header comment at line 5 and the case branch at line 51-54 both support --read-pinned-key, but the two usage lines printed on error (line 42 and line 58) only list '[--cross-branch|--repo-only] <plugin> <subpath>'. A user who runs the script with no args or with a missing plugin name sees a help message that hides one-third of the supported flags. Fix: update both usage strings to '[--cross-branch|--repo-only|--read-pinned-key] <plugin> <subpath>' to match the docstring and the actual parser.

findings_details.title: Branch-mode slug block duplicates the fallback tail inside _rq_resolve_state_key
findings_details.body: Lines 108-111 re-implement the exact fallback that already lives at lines 28-31 of lib/state-paths.sh (raw_branch = git rev-parse --abbrev-ref HEAD, slugify_branch, _rq_is_safe_key guard, 'unknown' sentinel). That is precisely the fourth-duplicate shape #448 was written to collapse — just one level deeper. The user's review question (#3) flags this block and the answer is: a small extraction reads cleaner and eliminates the duplication the PR is ostensibly about. Fix: extract the slug fallback into lib/state-paths.sh as _rq_current_branch_slug(), call it both from _rq_resolve_state_key (replacing lines 28-31 there) and from rq-state-path.sh's branch-mode arm (replacing lines 108-111). Result: one definition of 'how the branch slug is derived,' the pinned-key/branch-mode if/else in rq-state-path.sh collapses to two parallel one-liners, and the helper in state-paths.sh becomes self-composing.

findings_details.title: Fallback parity test omits adversarial cases present in canonical test
findings_details.body: test_repo_key_fallback.sh exercises only happy-path fixtures (https/ssh/file/empty/trailing-slash) but omits the three adversarial cases that test_repo_key.sh validates against the canonical lib: git@host:../evil/repo.git, git@host:-evil/repo.git, and the embedded-newline URL. The file's stated purpose is parity with lib/repo-key.sh, but parity that only covers the sunny path will not catch future drift in the inline fallback's traversal handling — which is exactly the class of bug the round-1 fallback-divergence finding was about. Fix: mirror the three adversarial blocks from test_repo_key.sh (lines 169-199) into test_repo_key_fallback.sh so the fallback gets the same safety assertions as the canonical lib.

findings_details.title: Inline _rq_derive_repo_key fallback lacks TODO breadcrumb to #448
findings_details.body: lib/quality-paths.sh line 51 carries a TODO(#448) pointing reviewers at the duplication-tracking issue for the non-collapsible fourth copy. The inline fallback in _lib_paths.sh (lines 26-54) is that fourth copy and is the one most likely to drift silently (it only runs when rq is not installed, so normal test runs never exercise it). It should carry the same TODO(#448) breadcrumb above the `_rq_is_safe_key` / `_rq_derive_repo_key` fallback definitions so a future reader landing here from `git blame` sees the tracking issue without having to cross-reference quality-paths.sh. Fix: add `# TODO(#448): this fallback is a byte-identical copy of lib/repo-key.sh. Parity is enforced by test_repo_key_fallback.sh.` above line 27.

findings_details.title: NUL-byte sanitizer check in scenario 8 is ineffective
findings_details.body: The loop iterates `$'\x00' $'\x08' $'\x1b' $'\x1f' $'\x7f'` and runs `grep -q "$b"` against the sanitized output. Under bash, `grep -q` with a NUL-byte pattern matches every input (grep treats the pattern as effectively empty), so the NUL iteration always sets `bad_byte=""` (the empty NUL value) — but since the final test is `[ -z "$bad_byte" ]`, an empty assignment is indistinguishable from 'no bad byte found'. The check passes for the wrong reason on NUL: a genuine NUL leak would not be caught. The other four byte classes (BS, ESC, unit-sep, DEL) are validated correctly. Fix: replace the grep-based check with a byte-presence test that tolerates NUL, e.g. `LC_ALL=C od -An -v -tx1 <<<"$existing" | tr -d ' \n' | grep -qE '00|08|1b|1f|7f' && bad_byte=detected`, or drop NUL from the loop since `_rq_owner_read`'s `tr -d '\000...'` already handles it and add a dedicated NUL-only assertion that reads the file via od.

findings_details.title: Scenario 10 tests the shell pattern, not rq-state-path.sh's actual cross-branch bypass
findings_details.body: The comment claims 'cross-branch mode — rq-state-path.sh --cross-branch skips check', but the subshell literally hand-codes the `if [ "$MODE" != 'cross-branch' ]` guard rather than invoking `rq-state-path.sh --cross-branch <plugin> <subpath>`. If a future refactor moves the guard, inverts the condition, or drops the `--repo-only` alias from the case statement in rq-state-path.sh, this test would continue to pass while the real script silently regresses. Fix: invoke the real script, e.g. `bash "$SCRIPT_DIR/rq-state-path.sh" --cross-branch myplug sub` inside a throwaway git worktree (or use `RQ_STATE_OWNER_DISABLE=0` + a fixture repo) and assert exit 0 plus no collision alert on stderr. At minimum add a second subscenario that exercises the `--repo-only` alias since the refactor advertises both flags route to MODE=cross-branch.

findings_details.title: `|| exit 1` inside function used in $(...) — `return 1` would be idiomatic
findings_details.body: `resolve_state_path` is invoked as `STATE_PATH=$(resolve_state_path)` at line 123 under `set -euo pipefail`. Verified empirically that `exit 1` inside the command-substitution subshell does propagate to the outer script (exit status 1), so this is not a bug. However, the function idiom everywhere else in rq (quality-paths.sh:52 uses `|| return 1`) is to `return` from a function and `exit` only at top-level. Using `exit 1` here couples the function to being called at top-level — if a future refactor calls `resolve_state_path` outside `$(...)` (e.g. assigning via nameref or calling for side effects), `exit 1` would terminate the caller unexpectedly. Fix: change `exit 1` to `return 1` on line 113; the existing `$(resolve_state_path)` caller with `set -e` already aborts on non-zero. Also applies to line 93 for symmetry.

findings_details.title: _verify_copy gives false-pass when new_root already contains files from a prior partial run
findings_details.body: The check 'dst_count >= src_count' compares the total file count in new_root against source. If a prior partial migration left files in new_root (e.g. tar succeeded, rm -rf failed), a subsequent run merges more files in and the check passes even if tar copied zero files this time (old dst_count already satisfied the threshold). The guard should count only the files that exist in new_root after the copy minus any that existed before, or use a dedicated temp dir and rename. Simplest fix: snapshot dst_count before the copy (pre_count) and verify dst_count - pre_count >= src_count.

findings_details.title: patch_proof leaves orphan tmp file in proof dir when jq fails
findings_details.body: mktemp is now called next to the target file (same filesystem, correct), but there is no trap to remove the temp file if the subsequent jq invocation fails. Under set -euo pipefail the script exits on jq failure, leaving qa-compare.XXXXXX in the proof directory. The old /tmp placement was self-cleaning (OS-level). Fix: add 'trap "rm -f $tmp" EXIT' immediately after the mktemp assignment.

findings_details.title: Concurrent mv can nest legacy dir inside new_root
findings_details.body: The check-then-mv at lines 46-51 is not atomic. Two concurrent hooks can both pass the `[ -d "$new_root" ]` check when new_root does not yet exist. Peer A then creates new_root via its mv; peer B's mv runs afterward. `mv old_root new_root` when new_root EXISTS as a directory moves old_root INTO new_root, producing `$HOME/.claude/state/<new_key>/<legacy_key>/...` — a nested stale copy of the migrated state. The trailing `2>/dev/null || true` silently swallows any error/oddity, so the user never sees this. Fix: either (a) use `mv -T` (GNU) / equivalent to force replace-or-fail and keep `|| true`, or (b) re-check `[ -d "$new_root" ]` immediately after the mv and rmdir/rename the nested dir if it appeared, or (c) gate the migration behind a flock on `$HOME/.claude/state/<new_key>.lock`. `mv -T` is the smallest safe change and matches the stated intent ('refuse to migrate when new_root exists').

findings_details.title: Symlink check is TOCTOU-prone
findings_details.body: `[ -L "$old_root" ] && return 0` at line 45 followed by `mv "$old_root" "$new_root"` at line 51 is a classic TOCTOU: a local attacker (or a racing peer) can swap `$old_root` between a regular dir and a symlink after the check but before the mv. Practical impact is low here because `$HOME/.claude/state/<legacy_key>` is user-owned and attacker would already need write access to the user's home. Note for completeness — not a blocker — but worth pairing with the flock suggested above.

findings_details.title: Branch-mode fallback diverges from helper on unsafe keys
findings_details.body: In branch-mode (lines 108-111), when `slugify_branch` produces an unsafe result, the code falls back to `branch_slug="unknown"` silently. The shared `_rq_resolve_state_key` helper does the same silently. Both pinned-key and branch-mode thus land on the literal string `unknown` as a state dir. This is intentional and matches the prior behavior, but since the finding list in issue #448 called out the inline block specifically: confirmed it uses the same `_rq_is_safe_key` validation as the helper and the fall-through key is identical. No behavioral regression. Flagging only so the reviewer/user can confirm this was intentional.

findings_details.title: Stale comment references removed _qp_derive_repo_key
findings_details.body: After issue #441 collapsed the three copies into lib/repo-key.sh::_rq_derive_repo_key, git-helpers.sh:52 still says 'Matches lib/quality-paths.sh::_qp_derive_repo_key's guard byte-for-byte'. The _qp_derive_repo_key symbol no longer exists — quality-paths.sh now sources repo-key.sh. Fix: update the comment to reference lib/repo-key.sh::_rq_derive_repo_key so future readers tracing the parity claim find the real function.

findings_details.title: Inline fallback is a second copy with no parity enforcement
findings_details.body: The fallback block at lines 24-51 re-implements _rq_is_safe_key and _rq_derive_repo_key verbatim for the standalone-install case. This is exactly the byte-identical-copy problem the PR claims to collapse: when lib/repo-key.sh gets a bug fix or URL-parser update, the therapist inline copy will silently diverge, and the parity test (test_quality_paths_parity.sh, test_repo_key.sh) only exercises the rq copy — therapist's fallback is never run in CI unless rq is absent. Two concrete failure modes: (1) a security fix to _rq_is_safe_key lands in rq but therapist keeps accepting poisoned keys on standalone installs; (2) a new URL scheme handled in _rq_derive_repo_key produces different state dirs under the fallback vs the sourced lib, fragmenting state across 'did the find succeed this session' coin flips. Fix options (pick one): (a) add a CI gate or test that sources the inline fallback under a stubbed find and runs it through test_repo_key.sh's matrix, asserting byte-identical output vs the canonical lib; (b) drop the fallback entirely and make therapist hard-depend on rq being installed (documented prereq); (c) vendor rq's repo-key.sh at install time into plugins/therapist/scripts/lib/ so there's exactly one on-disk copy, sourced unconditionally. Option (a) preserves standalone-install while making drift detectable; (b) is simpler if the standalone claim isn't actually load-bearing; (c) trades one fallback branch for a vendoring step. The current state — 'trust me, they match' with no mechanical enforcement — is the same trust model that produced the three-way duplication this PR exists to eliminate.

findings_details.title: find discovery may load stale/older rq copy over newer HOME install
findings_details.body: The discovery does: (1) find . -path '*/rq/scripts/lib/*' | head -1, then (2) only if that's empty, fall back to HOME with 'sort -V | tail -1'. The cwd pass has no version sort and takes the first hit. Scenarios that break: a worktree or repo containing a vendored or checked-in copy of plugins/rq/scripts/lib/repo-key.sh (e.g., this very repo — arqu-co/agent-plugins is the rq plugin's source tree, so running therapist from inside this repo will source the in-tree copy instead of the user's installed plugin version). If the in-tree copy is a work-in-progress, older, or from a different feature branch than the user's installed rq, therapist silently picks up whatever the cwd happens to contain. Secondary issue: 'find .' with no -maxdepth can be slow on large trees and, worse, will descend into node_modules / .venv / nested submodules, occasionally picking up a vendored copy from some unrelated project. Fix: prefer the HOME-installed plugin (the actual installed rq), and only fall back to cwd when no HOME copy exists, OR version-sort both candidate lists and take the highest. Also add -maxdepth (e.g., 6) and/or filter out node_modules/.venv so the search is bounded and deterministic. The current order privileges 'whatever source tree I'm standing in' over 'the plugin the user actually installed', which is backwards for a runtime discovery.

findings_details.title: CROSS-REFERENCE comment overstates what's duplicated
findings_details.body: The header says 'duplicated from rq-state-path.sh::resolve_state_path(). Any change to repo-key derivation, slugification, legacy-key migration, or branch-issue.json reading MUST be mirrored in rq-state-path.sh.' After #441, repo-key derivation is NO LONGER duplicated — both files source lib/repo-key.sh. Only legacy-key migration and pinned-key/slug logic remain duplicated. Leaving 'repo-key derivation' in the must-mirror list misleads future editors into thinking they need to update two places when they actually only need to edit repo-key.sh. Fix: trim the CROSS-REFERENCE list to the items still genuinely duplicated (legacy-key migration, slugification fallback, pinned-key reading), and note that repo-key derivation is now canonical in lib/repo-key.sh. rq-state-path.sh:134-137 already has the correct scoped wording — mirror it here.

findings_details.title: _rsp_resolve_branch_slug duplicates _qp_resolve_state_key
findings_details.body: quality-paths.sh::_qp_resolve_state_key (lines 56-70) and rq-state-path.sh::_rsp_resolve_branch_slug (lines 112-132) implement the same algorithm: read .state_key from branch-issue.json, validate with _rq_is_safe_key, echo warning on unsafe, fall back to slugified HEAD, final 'unknown' fallback. The only behavioral difference is the warning prefix ('quality-paths:' vs 'rq-state-path:') and that _rsp takes a mode arg to skip pinned-key lookup in branch mode. Since #441 just proved the three-way duplication problem by collapsing it, leaving this two-way duplicate in the same PR is inconsistent with the stated goal. Fix: extract into lib/repo-key.sh (or a new lib/state-key.sh) as _rq_resolve_state_key with a caller-supplied warning prefix; both resolvers source and call it. The parity test then reduces to asserting both callers produce identical output for identical inputs, which is what test_quality_paths_parity.sh already implicitly tests — but explicit single-sourcing eliminates the class of drift that required the test to exist. If the 'inlining for hot-path perf' rationale from quality-paths.sh header is genuinely load-bearing, benchmark sourcing-one-more-tiny-lib vs inlining and document the number — otherwise the perf claim is speculation protecting duplication.

findings_details.title: Test matrix misses several URL shapes the parser handles
findings_details.body: _rq_derive_repo_key has non-trivial branches not covered by test_repo_key.sh: (1) ssh://user@host/owner/repo (explicit ssh:// scheme, different from git@ shorthand — tests only cover git@ shorthand); (2) https URL WITHOUT .git suffix (common for 'gh repo clone' output); (3) URL with trailing slash 'https://github.com/acme/widgets/' (repo_part becomes empty string, owner_repo becomes 'acme/'); (4) multi-colon git@ URL like 'git@host:22:owner/repo' which the global ':→/' substitution was explicitly commented as handling. Tests pin 'global substitution' behavior in the comment but no test exercises a multi-colon URL — the comment is asserting behavior the tests don't verify. Fix: add cases for each shape above. Case (3) in particular may reveal a real bug — with trailing slash, owner_repo becomes 'acme/' and after //→- substitution 'acme-', which then passes _rq_is_safe_key and produces a different state dir than the canonical 'acme-widgets'. Worth verifying what today's code does before declaring intent.

findings_details.title: Function name prefix inconsistency — _th_ wraps calls to _rq_ names
findings_details.body: The therapist convention appears to be _th_ prefix (_th_source_repo_key, _resolve_therapist_dir, _clean_legacy_therapist_symlink — though the last two break the convention). After sourcing, the public symbols _rq_is_safe_key and _rq_derive_repo_key appear in therapist's namespace with the rq prefix. This is intentional (reusing rq's canonical names enables the fallback block to be byte-identical), but it's worth a sentence in the header comment so a future therapist-only contributor isn't confused seeing _rq_ symbols inside a therapist script and hunting for where they 'should be renamed'. Fix: either (a) add a one-line comment at top of the file documenting that _rq_* symbols are intentionally sourced from rq's lib; or (b) accept this is minor and don't bother. I'd do (a) since this file explicitly calls out the standalone-install constraint — making the convention explicit costs one line.
